title: std::array of class types, and brace elision

exercises.title: Student Roster Lookup
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Create a `std::array` of structs representing students and look up a student by index.

## Input

- Line 1: a single integer representing the 0-based index of the student to look up.

## Output

- If the index is valid (0 to 3 inclusive), print a single line:

```
<name> has grade <grade>
```

- If the index is out of range, print:

```
Invalid index
```

## Requirements

1. Define a struct `Student` at namespace scope with two members: `std::string_view name{}` and `int grade{}`.
2. In `main`, define a `constexpr std::array<Student, 4>` named `roster` containing these students in order: `{"Alice", 92}`, `{"Bob", 85}`, `{"Carol", 97}`, `{"Dave", 78}`.
3. Use brace elision (single outer braces) when initializing the array.
4. Read the index from `std::cin`. Validate it is in range `0` to `roster.size() - 1` inclusive.
5. Use `roster[index]` to access the element (not `.at()`).
6. Print the result in the exact format shown above.

exercises.title: Rectangle Area Report
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Store a collection of rectangles in a `std::array` of structs, then compute and print the area of each rectangle that has an area greater than or equal to a given threshold.

## Input

- Line 1: a single integer representing the minimum area threshold.

## Output

- For each rectangle whose area (width * height) is greater than or equal to the threshold, print one line:

```
<name>: <area>
```

- If no rectangles meet the threshold, print:

```
None
```

- Rectangles must be checked in the order they appear in the array.

## Requirements

1. Define a struct `Rect` at namespace scope with three members: `std::string_view name{}`, `int width{}`, `int height{}`.
2. In `main`, define a `constexpr std::array<Rect, 5>` named `rects` with these values in order: `{"A", 3, 4}`, `{"B", 10, 2}`, `{"C", 1, 1}`, `{"D", 5, 5}`, `{"E", 2, 3}`.
3. Use brace elision (single outer braces) when initializing the array.
4. Read the threshold from `std::cin`.
5. Loop through `rects` using a range-based for loop with `const auto&`.
6. For each rectangle, compute `width * height`. If the area is >= threshold, print it in the format shown.
7. Track whether any rectangle was printed. If none were, print `None`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- threshold 10 shows A, B, D
